board.title: Algorithms in Context
board.author: Julius Boateng
board.description: Algorithms are often discussed as tools for solving specific problems, but there is rich human history behind their creation. This puzzle explores the context behind several important algorithms.

clues.question: Heuristic pathfinding algorithm created at Stanford Research Institute in 1968 for the Shakey project; foundational to robotics and navigation systems
Lossless data compression algorithm using an optimal prefix code; created by an MIT student in 1951 for a final term paper
Fast Fourier Transform algorithm popularized by J. W. Cooley and John Tukey during the Cold War; later used in Soviet Union nuclear-test detection
Decision rule for minimizing the maximum possible loss; formulated by John von Neumann in 1928 for zero-sum game theory
Divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm created by Tony Hoare in 1959 at the National Physical Laboratory; also known as partition-exchange sort
Dynamic-programming decoding algorithm proposed in 1967 for noisy telecommunications links; later used for hidden Markov models
Public-key cryptography system created at MIT in 1977 by Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman; encryption relies on the difficulty of factoring large integers
Randomized sampling technique named after a casino district; Stanisław Ulam developed the method at Los Alamos during the Manhattan Project
Linear programming algorithm developed by George Dantzig in 1947; influenced by his wartime planning work for the U.S. Army Air Forces during WWII
Lossless data compression algorithm created by Abraham Lempel and Jacob Ziv in 1977; foundational to GIF and ZIP compression
Distributed consensus protocols introduced by Leslie Lamport in 1989 through "The Part-Time Parliament" paper
Dutch computer scientist who devised a foundational single-source shortest-path algorithm while sitting at a café in Amsterdam with his fiancée
